items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
Nagging doubts are tormenting me again...
Shpak has a tape recorder,
the ambassador has a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: Core comedic mechanism is mock-heroic/high-brow phrasing applied to petty suspicion, plus the parallel structure “У Шпака — …, у посла — …” meaning each LOST an item (not possession). The actual translation keeps the ‘doubts’ idea but shifts register to plain/colloquial (“Nagging doubts”) and, more importantly, mistranslates the second clause as possession (“has”), which breaks the logic of the callback (they no longer have them; they’re missing). This is a meaning error that also weakens the joke’s detective-like ‘connecting clues’ feel.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation:
Very nice, Tsar!
Very nice—hello, Tsar!

Tsar, very nice!
Hello, Tsar! Very nice!
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: The humor is the era-clash: Bunsha uses banal Soviet polite clichés and keeps reintroducing himself as “the Tsar,” with repetitive, awkward formality. The translation preserves repetition and the social-ritual awkwardness, but it drops the self-identifying ‘I’m the Tsar’ / definite-article feel and turns it into addressing the other person as “Tsar,” which changes the gag’s direction (it becomes flattery/address rather than self-presentation). Still readable and funny-ish, but less precise to the intended comic mechanism.

items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Matches the reference in meaning, register, and punch. Slightly more clipped than the reference but fully functional and in-character.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me, old woman, I’m sad.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: The line’s comic effect relies on elevated/archaic melancholy (“в печали”) contrasted with the mundane setting, plus the slightly grand, old-fashioned cadence. “I’m sad” is understandable but flattens the mock-archaic dignity; “in sorrow” (or similar) better preserves the regal tone. Not a plot-breaking error, but it reduces the stylistic contrast that carries the joke.

items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Meaning is mostly conveyed (he believes ‘demons’ have walled him in), but English needs an explicit subject/object to avoid sounding like he is describing a wall being built in general. The reference clarifies the victim perspective (“They’ve walled me in”), which is important because “демоны” are the agents. The current version is slightly abrupt/elliptical; still works in subtitles, but loses some clarity and the melodramatic complaint tone.

items.translation: That’s what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Conveys the religious attribution and the triumphant ‘miracle’ framing. Slightly less mock-biblical/ceremonial than “Behold the power…”, but still elevated enough to carry the joke in an atheist-modern context.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: The line is a set-piece catchphrase; the reference aims to preserve the MC/announcer vibe (opening the dance floor) rather than issuing a blunt command. “Everybody dance!” is understandable and energetic, but it reads as a direct imperative and loses the ‘host’/variety-show formulaic feel that makes it funnier in context.

items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Captures the pragmatic satisfaction and opportunistic tone (he’s pleased with his luck). “What perfect timing!” is a natural English equivalent and works well for the moment; it slightly shifts from “I dropped in well” to a more general timing comment, but the comedic beat remains.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation:
Black caviar, red caviar...
Yes! Overseas caviar...

Eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: Key creative requirement: avoid calling eggplant spread “caviar” in English, because it confuses viewers; the joke is the Soviet ‘eggplant caviar’ being treated as exotic. The current translation explicitly says “Overseas caviar,” then “Eggplant!”—this keeps the Russian term but creates exactly the confusion the reference warns against (English viewers may think eggplant is a type of caviar). Also, “Eggplant!” alone doesn’t signal ‘spread/dip’ and weakens the satirical inversion.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
Everything, everything I earned
through backbreaking labor,

all is lost: three tape recorders,
three movie cameras,

three domestic cigarette cases,
three suede jackets.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: The comedic effect depends on Shpak’s escalating inventory list and the specific beat where he awkwardly lands on “куртка… замшевая… три” (a single jacket item with a delayed ‘three’), revealing exaggeration and his own confusion. The translation smooths it into “three suede jackets,” removing the stutter/afterthought structure and the punch of the final standalone “Three.” That is a creative/structural loss, not just wording. Meaning is broadly correct (he’s inflating counts), but the joke’s timing is weakened.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And you’ll be cured too!

And you’ll be cured as well!

And I’ll be cured!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: The reference emphasizes the calm, sing-song resignation and the impersonal ‘they’ (the system/psych ward) as part of the darkly comic Soviet subtext. The translation keeps the repetition and basic meaning, but shifts to second-person/first-person without the impersonal agent (“they”), slightly reducing the institutional undertone. Still works and is funny; the loss is subtle.

summary.interpretation: 2.82 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.56 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
